light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.

generated_text.quick_read: Current trout fishing conditions on the Toccoa River are fair, supported by recent stocking and steady flow. High pressure and low cloud cover create technical fishing scenarios requiring finesse.
generated_text.best_window: Day 2 morning offers the best trout fishing window due to moderate sky cover and humidity with low wind gusts, aligning well with angler advice for shaded and deeper runs under bright, high-pressure conditions.
generated_text.current_conditions: Flow is 239 cfs with a gage height of 1.24 ft; water temperature and turbidity data are unavailable.
generated_text.weather_setup: Currently, the sky cover averages 28%, pressure is high at 30.17 inHg, stable with an unknown trend, light winds average 1.5 mph with gusts up to 8.1 mph, and there's a low 29% chance of light rain with 0.17 inches expected in the evening.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will favor shade, deeper runs, undercut banks, and structure, feeding cautiously under bright, high-pressure conditions. Use smaller natural flies, careful drifts, and less aggressive presentations, focusing on stocked-trout attractors like egg patterns, squirmy worms, and duracell nymphs; anglers should be cautious as fish may be cover-oriented and selective.
